content.text: Modern AI agents frequently need to read emails, create calendar events, push code commits, or post Slack messages on behalf of a human user. Doing that safely — without ever handling the user's password — requires a protocol that issues scoped, revocable, time-limited access credentials on behalf of that user. OAuth 2.0 is that protocol, and it is the de-facto standard across Google, GitHub, Microsoft, Slack, Salesforce, and hundreds of other platforms that agents interact with.
(defined in RFC 6749) is an authorization framework, not an authentication protocol. It defines how a
(typically a human user) can grant a
(your agent) limited access to resources hosted on a
(an API), without exposing the resource owner's credentials. The grant is mediated by an
that authenticates the user, collects consent, and issues short-lived

A critical distinction: OAuth 2.0 itself only specifies authorization — who may access what. It says nothing about who the user is. OpenID Connect (OIDC) is a thin identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 that adds an ID token (a signed JWT) carrying claims about the user (sub, email, name). Many providers — Google, Microsoft, Okta — expose both OAuth 2.0 and OIDC from the same authorization server, and agents typically use OIDC when they also need to know the user's identity.

OAuth 2.0 defines several grant types (flows) for different client capabilities and trust levels. The two flows that matter most for agents are Authorization Code (for agents with a backend that can keep a secret) and Authorization Code + PKCE (for public clients or any client following modern security best practice). The Client Credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine calls where no user delegation is involved.
— the human user who owns the data and approves the consent screen.
— your agent application, identified by a client_id registered with the provider.
— the provider's identity platform (Google accounts, GitHub OAuth apps, Microsoft Entra). It authenticates the user, captures consent, and mints tokens.
— the API that accepts access tokens (e.g., Gmail API, GitHub API). Often the same organisation as the authorization server but not always.

Before OAuth, delegated access meant giving a third-party application your username and password — a practice that is catastrophically risky. If the third party is breached, every account whose credentials it stored is compromised. The user cannot revoke access without changing their password, which revokes everyone. There is no way to restrict what the third party can do with those credentials.

OAuth eliminates all three problems. An agent stores tokens, not passwords. Each token carries an explicit scope list — the agent cannot do anything beyond what it was granted. The user can revoke a token from their account settings at any time, without affecting other applications or needing to rotate their password. The token has a short TTL (typically one hour for access tokens) so a leaked token has limited exposure.

For agentic AI in particular, OAuth matters because agents run asynchronously, often hours or days after the initial user interaction. The refresh token mechanism allows the agent to silently renew its access token when it expires, so a long-running task — summarise all emails from last week, review and merge PRs, draft calendar invites — completes without interrupting the user for repeated logins.
— agents never see or store user passwords; the attack surface is dramatically smaller.
— each token carries exactly the permissions the user approved: reading emails does not imply writing them.
— users can disconnect an agent from their account settings at any provider, immediately invalidating all its tokens.
— access tokens expire in minutes to hours; a stolen token has bounded damage.
— refresh tokens let long-running agents renew access silently, giving a smooth user experience for background tasks.
— scopes can be requested progressively; request only what is needed for each feature and add more later when the user enables new capabilities.
— libraries exist for every language; providers implement the same protocol, reducing integration friction.
— the authorization code flow needs the user to open a browser and approve a consent screen; fully headless agents need a setup phase or device-code flow.
— the agent must persist tokens per user, detect expiry, handle refresh, and deal with revoked refresh tokens gracefully.
— scope names, token lifetimes, refresh semantics (Google rotates refresh tokens; GitHub does not), and error codes differ across providers.
— connecting to many services means managing many token pairs and tracking which user has connected which provider.
— refresh tokens can themselves expire (usually after months of inactivity) or be invalidated by password changes, forcing re-authentication at inconvenient times.

The Authorization Code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for agents in 2024. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, RFC 7636) was originally designed for mobile apps without a secure backend, but current best practice mandates it for all clients — including those with a client_secret — because it prevents authorization code interception attacks even when the redirect URI is intercepted.
The client creates a cryptographically random code_verifier (43–128 characters from the URL-safe alphabet). It derives code_challenge = BASE64URL(SHA-256(code_verifier)).
Append query parameters: response_type=code, client_id, redirect_uri, scope (space-separated), state (random CSRF token), code_challenge, code_challenge_method=S256.
The agent's UI sends the user to this URL. The provider displays its login page and consent screen listing the requested scopes.
After consent, the provider redirects to redirect_uri with ?code=AUTH_CODE&state=.... The agent verifies state matches the session value (CSRF check) before proceeding.

The agent backend POSTs the code, code_verifier, client_id, client_secret (if confidential client), and grant_type=authorization_code to the provider's token endpoint. The provider verifies that SHA-256(code_verifier) equals the code_challenge it stored in step 2.
The token endpoint returns access_token, token_type=Bearer, expires_in (seconds), and optionally refresh_token and id_token. Store these encrypted, keyed by (user_id, provider).
Include Authorization: Bearer <access_token> on every request to the resource server.

Before each API call, check if the token is within a safety margin of expiry (e.g. < 60 seconds remaining). If so, POST grant_type=refresh_token with the refresh_token to get a new access_token without user interaction.

Access tokens are typically JWTs (though the format is provider-defined). A JWT has three base64url-encoded parts separated by dots: header (alg, kid), payload (iss, sub, aud, exp, iat, scope), and signature. The resource server validates the signature against the provider's public key (fetched from the JWKS endpoint) and rejects tokens where exp is in the past.
Refresh tokens are opaque strings — the agent should treat them as uninterpretable blobs, never attempt to decode them, and always store them with the same security level as a password.

When the agent itself — not a user — is the resource owner, use Client Credentials: POST client_id + client_secret + grant_type=client_credentials to the token endpoint and receive an access token scoped to the application. No user redirect is involved. This is appropriate for agents calling your own internal APIs, not user-data APIs like Gmail.
The diagram below shows the complete Authorization Code + PKCE flow, including the token refresh path that allows agents to continue operating after an access token expires.

attrs.alt: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code + PKCE flow diagram

content.text: Key observations from the diagram: steps 1–6 happen once per user per provider (the consent ceremony). Steps 7–8 repeat for every API call. Steps 9–10 happen silently in the background whenever the access token nears expiry, extending the agent's authorized session without requiring user interaction.
Use OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code whenever the agent needs to act on behalf of a specific human user at a third-party service. Concrete signals:
The data belongs to the user (their email, calendar, GitHub repos, Notion pages).
The provider supports OAuth 2.0 (practically every major SaaS does).
Actions must be attributable to and revocable by the user — compliance and audit requirements often mandate this.
The agent runs for extended periods (hours to days) — refresh tokens allow uninterrupted background operation.
Do not use Authorization Code OAuth when:
The agent is calling your own API — use API keys or service-account tokens instead.
There is no user context — use Client Credentials or service account JWT.
The provider offers a simpler PAT (Personal Access Token) model for developer tools — weigh the implementation complexity.

A complete OAuth implementation for an agent requires four components: a connect flow (initiate + callback), a token store (persist and retrieve per-user tokens), a refresh helper (called before every API request), and a generic API caller that orchestrates these pieces.

import hashlib
import os
import secrets
import time
from urllib.parse import urlencode
from typing import TypedDict

import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse

app = FastAPI()

# --- Config (load from env in production) ---
CLIENT_ID     = os.environ["GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID"]
CLIENT_SECRET = os.environ["GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET"]
REDIRECT_URI  = "https://my-agent.example.com/auth/google/callback"
AUTH_URL      = "https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth"
TOKEN_URL     = "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token"
SCOPES        = [
    "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.readonly",
    "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events",
    "openid",
    "email",
]

# --- Simple in-memory stores (use Redis/DB in production) ---
_sessions: dict[str, dict] = {}   # session_id -> {state, code_verifier, user_id}
_tokens:   dict[str, dict] = {}   # user_id    -> {access_token, refresh_token, expires_at}


class TokenRecord(TypedDict):
    access_token:  str
    refresh_token: str
    expires_at:    float          # Unix timestamp


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1.  Initiate: build the authorization URL and redirect
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
@app.get("/auth/google/connect")
def google_connect(request: Request, user_id: str):
    # PKCE
    code_verifier  = secrets.token_urlsafe(96)          # ~128 chars
    digest         = hashlib.sha256(code_verifier.encode()).digest()
    code_challenge = (
        __import__("base64").urlsafe_b64encode(digest).rstrip(b"=").decode()
    )
    state      = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    session_id = secrets.token_urlsafe(16)

    _sessions[session_id] = {
        "state": state,
        "code_verifier": code_verifier,
        "user_id": user_id,
    }

    params = {
        "client_id":             CLIENT_ID,
        "response_type":         "code",
        "redirect_uri":          REDIRECT_URI,
        "scope":                 " ".join(SCOPES),
        "state":                 state,
        "code_challenge":        code_challenge,
        "code_challenge_method": "S256",
        "access_type":           "offline",   # Google-specific: request refresh token
        "prompt":                "consent",   # Force consent so refresh token is issued
    }
    response = RedirectResponse(url=f"{AUTH_URL}?{urlencode(params)}")
    response.set_cookie("oauth_session", session_id, httponly=True, secure=True)
    return response


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2.  Callback: verify state, exchange code for tokens, persist
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
@app.get("/auth/google/callback")
def google_callback(request: Request, code: str, state: str):
    session_id = request.cookies.get("oauth_session")
    session    = _sessions.pop(session_id, None)

    if not session or session["state"] != state:
        return {"error": "invalid state — possible CSRF"}

    resp = httpx.post(TOKEN_URL, data={
        "grant_type":    "authorization_code",
        "code":          code,
        "redirect_uri":  REDIRECT_URI,
        "client_id":     CLIENT_ID,
        "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET,
        "code_verifier": session["code_verifier"],
    })
    resp.raise_for_status()
    token_data = resp.json()

    user_id = session["user_id"]
    _tokens[user_id] = {
        "access_token":  token_data["access_token"],
        "refresh_token": token_data.get("refresh_token"),
        "expires_at":    time.time() + token_data["expires_in"] - 60,  # 60 s safety margin
    }
    return {"status": "connected", "user_id": user_id}


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3.  Refresh helper — call before every API request
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def get_valid_access_token(user_id: str) -> str:
    record = _tokens.get(user_id)
    if not record:
        raise RuntimeError(f"User {user_id} has not connected Google")

    if time.time() < record["expires_at"]:
        return record["access_token"]          # still valid

    # Refresh
    resp = httpx.post(TOKEN_URL, data={
        "grant_type":    "refresh_token",
        "refresh_token": record["refresh_token"],
        "client_id":     CLIENT_ID,
        "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET,
    })
    if resp.status_code == 400:
        del _tokens[user_id]                   # token revoked — need re-consent
        raise RuntimeError(f"Refresh token revoked for {user_id}; re-authentication required")
    resp.raise_for_status()
    new_data = resp.json()

    record["access_token"] = new_data["access_token"]
    record["expires_at"]   = time.time() + new_data["expires_in"] - 60
    if "refresh_token" in new_data:            # Some providers rotate refresh tokens
        record["refresh_token"] = new_data["refresh_token"]
    return record["access_token"]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4.  Agent tool — Gmail search
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def search_gmail(user_id: str, query: str, max_results: int = 10) -> list[dict]:
    token = get_valid_access_token(user_id)
    resp  = httpx.get(
        "https://gmail.googleapis.com/gmail/v1/users/me/messages",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        params={"q": query, "maxResults": max_results},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json().get("messages", [])
Example 1 — Minimal PKCE helper (Python, no framework)

"""
Minimal PKCE code_verifier / code_challenge generator.
Works with any OAuth 2.0 provider that supports S256.
"""
import base64
import hashlib
import secrets


def generate_pkce_pair() -> tuple[str, str]:
    """Return (code_verifier, code_challenge)."""
    code_verifier = secrets.token_urlsafe(96)   # 128-char URL-safe string
    digest = hashlib.sha256(code_verifier.encode("ascii")).digest()
    code_challenge = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(digest).rstrip(b"=").decode("ascii")
    return code_verifier, code_challenge


if __name__ == "__main__":
    verifier, challenge = generate_pkce_pair()
    print("code_verifier :", verifier)
    print("code_challenge:", challenge)
    # Verify: re-derive should match
    digest2 = hashlib.sha256(verifier.encode()).digest()
    assert base64.urlsafe_b64encode(digest2).rstrip(b"=").decode() == challenge
    print("PKCE round-trip: OK")
Example 2 — GitHub OAuth token exchange (JavaScript / Node.js)

Consider a personal AI assistant that helps users manage email and calendar across multiple accounts. Each user connects one or more Google workspaces and a Microsoft 365 account. The agent runs daily background jobs: summarise unread emails, detect scheduling conflicts, draft replies.
Architecture decisions driven by OAuth requirements:

— PostgreSQL table with columns (user_id, provider, access_token_enc, refresh_token_enc, expires_at). access_token_enc and refresh_token_enc are encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a key stored in AWS KMS. A unique row exists per (user_id, provider) combination.

— every tool function calls ensure_fresh_token(user_id, provider) first. This checks expires_at and refreshes if within 120 seconds of expiry. A Redis distributed lock prevents parallel refreshes for the same user from competing.
— if the API returns 401 or the refresh endpoint returns invalid_grant, the agent writes a flag to the token record and sends the user a notification to reconnect.
— the token store lookup always joins on user_id; there is no global token variable. Isolation is enforced at the DB query level, not just application logic.

Omitting the state parameter — or setting it to a predictable constant — opens a CSRF attack on the OAuth callback: an attacker can trick a user's browser into visiting the callback URL with an attacker-controlled code, connecting the victim's agent account to the attacker's provider account.

# WRONG — state is static or absent
auth_url = f"{AUTH_URL}?client_id={CLIENT_ID}&code_challenge={challenge}&..."

# CORRECT — generate a cryptographically random state per session
state = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
session["oauth_state"] = state
auth_url = f"{AUTH_URL}?...&state={state}"

# In callback — always verify before using the code
if request.args["state"] != session.pop("oauth_state", None):
    abort(400, "invalid state")

Asking for gmail.modify when only gmail.readonly is needed, or requesting repo (full repository access) when only repo:status is required, violates the principle of least privilege. Users and corporate IT policies increasingly reject consent for broad scopes, and a compromised agent token with write access causes far more damage than a read-only one.

Refresh tokens are long-lived, high-value credentials. Storing them in a plaintext database column means a SQL injection or a database backup leak permanently compromises every connected user account. Always encrypt at the application layer (Fernet, AES-GCM) in addition to database-level encryption.
An access token may be revoked or may expire slightly earlier than expected due to clock skew. Always retry on 401 with a fresh token before surfacing an error to the user or failing the agent task.

Google only returns a refresh_token on the first consent, or when prompt=consent is included. An agent that requests offline access without forcing consent will fail to get a refresh token for returning users, causing silent failures when the access token expires.
— even for confidential clients. It adds a meaningful layer of protection against authorization code interception at negligible implementation cost.
— derive the scope list from the tools actually registered for a user's session, not a universal superset. Prefer read-only scopes and add write scopes only when a write tool is explicitly enabled.
— check expiry 60–120 seconds before the deadline, not at the exact expiry. Avoids race conditions in long API calls.
— use envelope encryption: an application-layer key (Fernet or AES-256-GCM) wraps the token data; the master key lives in a KMS (AWS KMS, GCP Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault).
— when refresh fails with invalid_grant, delete the token record and notify the user through your application, providing a direct reconnect link.
— log when a user connects and disconnects a provider, including the approved scope list. Useful for compliance and debugging.
Isolate tokens by user and provider
— every database lookup includes user_id and provider in the WHERE clause. Never pass tokens across users in agent execution context.
— register the exact redirect_uri with the provider. Wildcard or open-ended URIs enable redirect URI hijacking attacks.

Token refresh is a synchronous network call that adds latency to every agent tool invocation after expiry. The most effective mitigation is proactive background refresh: a scheduled job or a cache-aside pattern refreshes tokens 5 minutes before they expire, so the tool call finds a fresh token without blocking.

Under high concurrency — many parallel agent threads for the same user — a thundering herd of refresh calls can hit the provider's rate limits. Use a distributed lock (Redis SETNX with a short TTL) so only one thread executes the refresh; others wait and then read the newly cached token.
with the TTL from expires_in. This avoids database lookups on every API call.
— when an agent job starts, pre-load all provider tokens for the relevant user. Eliminates per-call DB round-trips.
— a single OAuth flow can request all needed scopes for all tools in one consent screen; do not make the user go through multiple flows.
— track refresh failure rates per provider. A spike indicates a provider outage or a change in token policy.

Provider rate limits on the token endpoint vary. Google's OAuth 2.0 token endpoint allows approximately 10,000 requests per 100 seconds per project. At scale, a background refresh pattern that keeps all active user tokens current avoids hitting this limit during peak usage.
— official Google OAuth library; handles token refresh automatically for Google APIs.
— framework-agnostic OAuth 1/2 and OIDC library; works with Flask, Django, FastAPI, Starlette. Highly recommended for custom OAuth integrations.
— wraps the requests library with OAuth 1/2 session support.
— JWT decoding and validation for access token inspection.

# Using authlib to build an OAuth client (FastAPI example)
from authlib.integrations.starlette_client import OAuth

oauth = OAuth()
oauth.register(
    name="google",
    client_id=CLIENT_ID,
    client_secret=CLIENT_SECRET,
    server_metadata_url="https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration",
    client_kwargs={"scope": "openid email https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.readonly"},
)

# In your route:
# redirect = await oauth.google.authorize_redirect(request, REDIRECT_URI)
# token    = await oauth.google.authorize_access_token(request)
— full-featured auth for Next.js; built-in adapters for 60+ providers including Google, GitHub, Microsoft.
— certified OpenID Connect relying party library for Node.js; low-level but RFC-compliant.
— Google's official Node.js client; handles OAuth flow and token refresh automatically.
AWS Secrets Manager / GCP Secret Manager
— store client_secret and token encryption keys; rotate without deployment.
— managed databases with row-level security for multi-tenant token storage.
— managed OAuth infrastructure; they store tokens and expose an API, eliminating the need to build and secure your own token store.
OAuth 2.0 is the right tool when user delegation is needed, but other credential models are better suited for different agent patterns.

Authorization servers must validate the redirect_uri exactly against the registered URI. As a client, register the most specific URI possible. Avoid dynamic redirect URIs that include user-provided path segments — a malicious redirect_uri can direct the authorization code to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
Token leakage via browser history and logs

Authorization codes appear in query strings and can be captured in browser history, server access logs, and referrer headers. Always use the fragment-based response mode (#code=...) or ensure logs strip authorization codes. Access tokens must never appear in URLs — always in the Authorization header of server-side requests.

The state parameter is the primary CSRF defense on the OAuth callback. Use a cryptographically random value generated with secrets.token_urlsafe and stored in the user's session. Single-use is essential: pop the state from the session when you consume it so replay attacks fail.

A refresh token is semantically equivalent to a long-lived password for the connected account. Apply the same controls: encrypt with a per-deployment key managed in a KMS, hash a fingerprint for audit lookup, and log access. Include the token's expiry in the encrypted payload so the check does not require decryption for most requests.

Some providers (Microsoft, Okta) rotate the refresh token on every use — each refresh call returns a new refresh_token and the old one is immediately invalidated. Implement refresh token rotation handling: always persist the new refresh_token returned in the response, and never assume the stored refresh_token is still valid after a refresh call.

The token endpoint can be unavailable. Implement exponential backoff with jitter on refresh failures caused by 5xx responses or network timeouts. Distinguish transient failures (retry) from permanent invalidation (invalid_grant — stop retrying and notify the user). Cache the last known good token; in read-only scenarios, a briefly stale access token may be preferable to a hard failure.
Always validate the iss, aud, and exp claims in ID tokens (OIDC). Never trust an unvalidated JWT. Use a library that implements RFC 7519 validation — do not write JWT verification from scratch.
OAuth 2.0 replaces password sharing with scoped, revocable, time-limited access tokens — the fundamental security improvement for agent-to-service integration.
Use Authorization Code + PKCE for all user-delegated agent tool integrations; never skip PKCE even for confidential clients.
Always validate the state parameter on the OAuth callback to prevent CSRF attacks; use cryptographically random, single-use state values.
Persist refresh tokens encrypted at rest using application-layer AES-256-GCM with KMS-managed keys; treat them with the same security as passwords.
Refresh access tokens proactively (60–120 seconds before expiry) to avoid latency spikes; use a distributed lock under high concurrency to prevent thundering-herd refreshes.
Handle invalid_grant responses from the refresh endpoint explicitly — delete the token record and prompt the user to reconnect rather than failing silently.
Request minimum scopes at consent; defer write scopes until a write tool is actually enabled to improve user trust and reduce blast radius.
Some providers rotate refresh tokens on every use — always persist the new refresh_token returned by the token endpoint, even when you did not expect one.
Managed auth providers (Auth0, Clerk, WorkOS) can handle the entire token lifecycle, eliminating the need to build and secure a custom token store for most production deployments.
